goal_title_suggestions: Drop the weight and keep it off
Get lean through consistent daily action

variants: Download MyFitnessPal right now and create your free account.
Open the App Store, install MyFitnessPal, and complete the account setup.
Get MyFitnessPal on your phone and register your account before you do anything else today.
tasks.why: You need a tracking tool before you can measure anything — this is the foundation.

variants: Step on a scale right now, note your exact weight, and log it in MyFitnessPal under 'Check-In'.
Weigh yourself this morning, record the number in MyFitnessPal's weight tracker, and screenshot it.
Get your baseline weight on the scale today and enter it into MyFitnessPal — this is your starting line.
tasks.why: You cannot track progress without a starting number — weigh in now.

variants: Go to tdeecalculator.net, enter your stats (age, height, weight, activity level), and write down your TDEE and your target calories (TDEE minus 400).
Open tdeecalculator.net, fill in every field honestly, and record two numbers: your maintenance calories and your deficit target (subtract 400 from TDEE).
Calculate your TDEE at tdeecalculator.net right now — enter real numbers, not optimistic ones — then subtract 400 and save both figures somewhere you'll see them.
tasks.why: Your calorie target is the single number that drives fat loss — you need it today.

variants: Set your calorie and protein goals in MyFitnessPal: calories = your TDEE minus 400, protein = 0.7 times your bodyweight in pounds.
Open MyFitnessPal settings, enter the calorie target you calculated yesterday, and set protein to 0.7 grams per pound of your bodyweight.
Configure MyFitnessPal with yesterday's numbers: plug in your deficit target calories and set protein goal to 0.7g x your bodyweight in lbs.
tasks.why: Protein target and calorie ceiling are your two non-negotiable daily numbers.

variants: Log everything you eat for breakfast today in MyFitnessPal — search each item, enter accurate portions, and confirm the macros look right.
Open MyFitnessPal and log your breakfast right now with exact amounts — use the barcode scanner or search for each item.
Before you finish your morning, have every breakfast item entered in MyFitnessPal with real gram counts, not guesses.
tasks.why: Logging breakfast builds the habit — the first meal of the day is the easiest to capture.

variants: Go for a 20-minute walk today — outside, no stops, brisk pace, and log it in MyFitnessPal under Exercise when you get back.
Block 20 minutes and walk at a pace where you could talk but prefer not to — then log the walk in MyFitnessPal.
Do a 20-minute brisk walk right now or before bed — it counts, log it in MyFitnessPal when done.
tasks.why: Daily movement is the second lever of fat loss — start with a walk, keep it simple.

variants: Log your lunch and dinner from yesterday in MyFitnessPal if you haven't already — close the day out completely.
Open MyFitnessPal and finish logging yesterday: every meal, every snack, everything — then close the diary.
Complete yesterday's MyFitnessPal diary right now — fill in any missing meals so you have a full 24-hour picture.
tasks.why: A closed diary day tells you the truth about your intake — don't leave gaps.

variants: Look at yesterday's protein total in MyFitnessPal and identify one meal you could swap to add 20g of protein — write it down.
Check your protein from yesterday in MyFitnessPal, note the gap from your target, and pick one specific higher-protein food to add today.
Review yesterday's macro breakdown in MyFitnessPal, find where your protein fell short, and decide on one concrete food swap for today.
tasks.why: Protein is the top priority macro — identify where you're losing points and fix it today.

variants: Do 10 pushups, 15 bodyweight squats, and a 30-second plank right now — rest 60 seconds and repeat once more.
Complete 2 rounds of: 10 pushups, 15 squats, 30-second plank — rest 60 seconds between rounds, no equipment needed.
Right now, run 2 circuits back to back: 10 pushups, 15 squats, 30-second plank, 60 seconds rest — then log it in MyFitnessPal.
tasks.why: Bodyweight strength training preserves muscle during fat loss — start with a minimal circuit.

variants: Log today's breakfast in MyFitnessPal before you finish eating — not after, not tonight, right now while it's in front of you.
Scan or search every item in today's breakfast into MyFitnessPal before you leave the table.
Enter breakfast into MyFitnessPal with exact weights or portions before the meal is cleared — make logging the last step of eating.
tasks.why: Logging in the moment is 10x more accurate than logging from memory later.

variants: Plan your lunch and dinner for today in MyFitnessPal right now — enter the foods before you eat them so you can see the calorie total in advance.
Pre-log tonight's dinner and today's lunch in MyFitnessPal before noon so you can adjust if you're running over calories.
Open MyFitnessPal and fill in your planned meals for the rest of today — pre-logging keeps you from blowing the budget at dinner.
tasks.why: Pre-logging meals is the single highest-leverage habit for staying under your calorie target.

variants: Walk for 25 minutes at a brisk pace — step outside right now and cover as much ground as you can.
Do a 25-minute walk today, no distractions, brisk enough that you're breathing slightly harder than normal.
Get out and walk 25 minutes today — pick up the pace, cover ground, and log it when you're done.
tasks.why: Extending your walk by 5 minutes is the easiest way to increase your daily deficit.

variants: Take a photo of every meal today — breakfast, lunch, dinner — before you eat it.
Photograph each meal before you eat it today — a simple food log photo gives you something to reference later.
Snap a quick photo of each meal today before the first bite — you'll use these to verify your logging accuracy.
tasks.why: Visual food logs reveal portion distortions that numeric logging misses.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 3 rounds of 12 pushups, 20 squats, 10 lunges each leg, 45-second plank — rest 60 seconds between rounds.
Complete 3 rounds: 12 pushups, 20 squats, 10 lunges per leg, 45-sec plank — rest 60 seconds between each round, no gear needed.
Run 3 circuits right now: 12 pushups, 20 squats, 10 lunges each leg, 45-second plank, 60 seconds rest — then log it.
tasks.why: Adding a round and extra reps grows your strength baseline — progress must be measurable.

variants: Close today's MyFitnessPal diary completely — log every meal, every snack, every drink with calories, and check your protein total against your goal.
Before 9pm, have your full day logged in MyFitnessPal: breakfast, lunch, dinner, snacks, drinks — check protein and calories against targets.
Complete your full MyFitnessPal diary for today before bedtime: every item, every portion, and verify you hit protein within 10g of your target.
tasks.why: Closing the diary daily is the habit that separates people who lose weight from those who don't.

variants: Check MyFitnessPal right now and look at your average daily calories for the past 5 days — note whether you're actually hitting your target.
Open MyFitnessPal's nutrition dashboard and read your 5-day calorie average — write down the number.
Pull up your MyFitnessPal weekly summary and find your average calorie intake for days 1-5 — screenshot it.
tasks.why: The 5-day average is the real signal — one-day swings don't matter, averages do.

variants: Walk 25 minutes today and count your steps — write the step count in your notes app when you finish.
Do your 25-minute walk and check your phone's health app for total step count at the end of the walk — note it.
Walk 25 minutes brisk pace and record your step count from your phone pedometer — this is your day-6 baseline.
tasks.why: Tracking steps gives you an objective movement number beyond just 'I walked today'.

variants: Identify 3 high-protein meals you can make in under 10 minutes using what you already have at home — write all 3 down with their rough protein count.
Open your fridge and pantry right now, list 3 quick high-protein meals you can build from what's already there, and estimate the protein in each.
Write down 3 fast high-protein meals (10 minutes or less to make) you could realistically eat this week — include approximate protein grams for each.
tasks.why: Having a short list of go-to high-protein meals eliminates the decision that kills diets.

variants: Step on the scale this morning, record your weight in MyFitnessPal, and compare it to day 1 — note the difference, positive or negative.
Weigh in this morning, log it in MyFitnessPal, and write down the delta from your day-1 starting weight.
Do your week-1 weigh-in right now: scale, MyFitnessPal log, compare to day 1 number, and note any change.
tasks.why: Weekly weigh-ins track the trend — one week is your first real data point.

variants: Review your MyFitnessPal diary for days 1-6: count how many days you fully closed the diary and note your average protein against your target.
Open MyFitnessPal and audit the past 6 days: which days were the diary closed, what was your best protein day, and where did you miss?
Check MyFitnessPal's weekly report for days 1-6 — identify your best day, your worst day, and the one habit change that would have the biggest impact this week.
tasks.why: Weekly review shows you where your system is leaking — fix one thing, not everything.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 3 rounds of 15 pushups, 20 squats, 12 lunges each leg, 45-second plank — rest 60 seconds between rounds.
Complete 3 rounds: 15 pushups, 20 squats, 12 lunges per leg, 45-sec plank — rest 60 seconds, track your reps in a notes app.
Run 3 strength circuits today: 15 pushups, 20 squats, 12 lunges each leg, 45-second plank — rest 60 sec, log every rep count.
tasks.why: Week 1 ends with a strength session — add reps from day 3 to show progression.

variants: Log breakfast in MyFitnessPal before you finish eating — use the barcode scanner for packaged items and weigh anything you can.
Scan or weigh breakfast and log it in MyFitnessPal immediately — prioritize accuracy over speed today.
Before your breakfast plate is clear, have it fully entered in MyFitnessPal with accurate weights, not estimated portions.
tasks.why: Accuracy in logging is the discipline that makes the data meaningful.

variants: Drink a full glass of water before every meal today — set reminders on your phone for each meal time if needed.
Set a phone alarm for each of your 3 main meals today with the note: 'Drink water first' — execute it every time.
Before breakfast, lunch, and dinner today, drink 8oz of water — set three phone reminders right now to enforce it.
tasks.why: Water before meals reduces calorie intake naturally and builds a sustainable hunger-management habit.

variants: Walk 30 minutes today at a brisk pace — check your step count when you finish and write it in your notes.
Do a 30-minute brisk walk, note the step count from your phone when you get back, and log the walk in MyFitnessPal.
Get out for 30 minutes of walking today, push the pace slightly more than last week, and record your step count.
tasks.why: Week 2 bumps the walk to 30 minutes — small increases compound over 90 days.

variants: Pre-log your lunch and dinner in MyFitnessPal before noon today — build the whole day's plan before you eat it.
Open MyFitnessPal this morning and fill in every meal you plan to eat today — see the totals before the day happens.
Fill in your complete meal plan for today in MyFitnessPal before you leave the house — know your calorie budget before it's spent.
tasks.why: Pre-logging turns passive tracking into active eating decisions.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 3 rounds of 15 pushups, 25 squats, 12 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — log your reps after each round.
Complete 3 rounds: 15 pushups, 25 squats, 12 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — rest 60 seconds, record actual reps for each exercise.
Run 3 circuits: 15 pushups, 25 squats, 12 lunges each leg, 60-second plank, 60-sec rest — write down exact reps so you can beat them next session.
tasks.why: Incrementally harder circuits build the muscle that raises your resting metabolism.

variants: Identify and remove one high-calorie item from your home today — a snack, a drink, or a condiment — and replace it with a lower-calorie substitute.
Go through your pantry or fridge right now, pull out the one item most likely to blow your daily budget, and swap it for something with fewer calories.
Find one food in your kitchen that consistently kills your calorie target, throw it out or move it out of sight, and buy or prepare a lower-calorie swap today.
tasks.why: Removing friction from your environment beats willpower every single time.

variants: Log today's breakfast in MyFitnessPal and check your protein total so far — note whether you're front-loading protein or leaving it all to dinner.
Enter breakfast into MyFitnessPal and look at your current protein number — front-loading protein before noon keeps you full and on target.
Log breakfast in MyFitnessPal immediately, then check how much protein you've already hit for the day — aim for at least 30g by mid-morning.
tasks.why: Front-loading protein in the first half of the day reduces afternoon cravings.

variants: Walk 30 minutes today and make it a power walk — arms moving, fast pace, no phone in hand — then log it.
Go for a 30-minute power walk right now: arms pumping, fast stride, intentional breathing — log it in MyFitnessPal when done.
Do 30 minutes of intentional walking today — faster than comfortable, arms actively swinging — and record the step count.
tasks.why: Intensity in walking increases calorie burn without adding time.

variants: Cook or assemble a high-protein meal tonight with at least 35g of protein — log it in MyFitnessPal and write down the recipe in 3 lines.
Make one high-protein dinner tonight (35g+ protein), log every ingredient in MyFitnessPal, and note the meal name and protein count for future reference.
Prepare a dinner with at least 35g protein this evening, scan every ingredient into MyFitnessPal, and screenshot the completed diary entry.
tasks.why: Building a catalog of easy high-protein meals solves the hardest daily problem: what to eat.

variants: Check MyFitnessPal right now and confirm you closed yesterday's diary — if not, fill in what you can from memory and close it.
Open MyFitnessPal and verify yesterday is fully logged — patch any missing entries and close the diary before starting today.
Before logging today, make sure yesterday's MyFitnessPal diary is complete — close it out, then move to today.
tasks.why: Consistency in closing the diary is more important than perfection in any single day.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 3 rounds of 15 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — beat your day-9 reps by at least one.
Complete 3 rounds: 15 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — aim to beat day 9's total reps on at least one exercise.
Run 3 circuits: 15 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — your goal today is to post higher rep counts than day 9.
tasks.why: Progressive overload applies to bodyweight training — more reps equals more muscle stimulus.

variants: Drink your first 16oz of water before breakfast today and track your total water intake in MyFitnessPal or a notes app — aim for 64oz by end of day.
Start today with 16oz of water immediately after waking, then track every glass in your notes app — hit 64oz total before 8pm.
Drink 16oz of water before your first meal and use MyFitnessPal's water tracker to log every additional glass — target 64oz or more today.
tasks.why: Adequate hydration reduces false hunger signals and supports every fat-loss mechanism.

variants: Pre-log tomorrow's meals in MyFitnessPal tonight — plan a full day in advance and make sure you hit protein and calorie targets on paper.
Open MyFitnessPal tonight and build tomorrow's full meal plan — every meal, every snack, calories and protein both under control.
Before bed tonight, fill in tomorrow's entire MyFitnessPal diary — eating tomorrow's plan on paper before it happens keeps you accountable.
tasks.why: Planning the next day the night before is the most reliable fat-loss habit there is.

variants: Walk 30 minutes and finish the last 5 minutes at your fastest walking pace possible — track the step count when done.
Do your 30-minute walk and sprint-finish the final 5 minutes at your top walking speed — no running required, just max walking pace.
Take your 30-minute walk today and dial up the intensity for the last 5 minutes — push your pace as hard as you can while still walking.
tasks.why: Ending walks at higher intensity trains your cardiovascular system progressively.

variants: Search MyFitnessPal for your top 3 most-logged foods and look up their protein content — swap out the lowest-protein one for a higher-protein alternative this week.
Open MyFitnessPal, find your 3 most frequently eaten foods, check the protein per serving for each, and identify the weakest one to replace.
Look at your most common foods in MyFitnessPal right now — pick the one with the worst protein-to-calorie ratio and decide what you'll eat instead starting tomorrow.
tasks.why: Upgrading your most frequent foods has a compounding effect on protein intake.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 4 rounds of 15 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — one more round than before.
Complete 4 full rounds: 15 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — rest 60 seconds between rounds, track every rep.
Run 4 circuits today: 15 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank, 60 seconds rest — an extra round is your progression.
tasks.why: Adding a round to your circuit is a clean, measurable increase in training volume.

variants: Log every drink you consume today in MyFitnessPal — coffee, juice, soda, alcohol, protein shakes — and see what liquid calories add up to.
Track every single beverage today in MyFitnessPal including black coffee, tea, and flavored drinks — total your liquid calorie count at end of day.
Open MyFitnessPal and log every drink that passes your lips today — most people are shocked by liquid calorie totals when they see them.
tasks.why: Liquid calories are the most commonly underestimated source of excess intake.

variants: Cook one batch of protein today — 4-5 servings of chicken breast, ground turkey, eggs, or canned tuna — and store it so you can use it in tomorrow's meals.
Prepare 4-5 servings of your preferred protein source right now: cook it, portion it, refrigerate it — this is your protein safety net for the next 2 days.
Batch cook one protein source today (chicken, turkey, eggs, tuna) into 4-5 portions and put them in the fridge — remove the excuse to skip protein tomorrow.
tasks.why: Prepped protein removes the friction that causes off-plan eating when you're tired.

variants: Step on the scale this morning, log it in MyFitnessPal, and compare to both day 1 and day 7 — write down the trend.
Weigh in, enter the number in MyFitnessPal, and track your 2-week trend: day 1, day 7, today — note whether the line is moving.
Do your week-2 weigh-in: scale, MyFitnessPal entry, and a written comparison of day 1 vs day 7 vs today to see your trajectory.
tasks.why: Two-week trend tells you whether your current approach is working — the data doesn't lie.

variants: Review your past 7 days in MyFitnessPal: calculate your average daily protein, average calories, and number of fully closed diary days — write all 3 down.
Pull up MyFitnessPal's week 2 data and record these 3 numbers: average daily calories, average daily protein, days the diary was fully closed.
Audit week 2 in MyFitnessPal right now — your 7-day calorie average, your 7-day protein average, and your diary completion rate — and note them.
tasks.why: Weekly review identifies your leverage point — fix the one number that's most off-target.

variants: Walk 30 minutes today, then write one sentence in your notes app describing how your energy, hunger, and consistency feel after 2 weeks.
Do your 30-minute walk, then open your notes and write a 2-week check-in: energy level, hunger patterns, and what's working in your routine.
After your 30-minute walk today, spend 3 minutes writing a brief week-2 journal entry: what's changed physically, what habits are sticking, what needs work.
tasks.why: Self-assessment at week 2 keeps you adapting your approach instead of blindly repeating it.

variants: Log breakfast in MyFitnessPal before 9am today and make sure it contains at least 25g of protein.
Have breakfast logged in MyFitnessPal by 9am — verify the protein count is at least 25g before you move on.
Get breakfast into MyFitnessPal this morning before 9am and confirm it hits 25g protein minimum — adjust if needed.
tasks.why: A high-protein breakfast before 9am sets up your hunger and energy for the entire day.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 4 rounds of 17 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — track your reps round by round.
Complete 4 rounds: 17 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — log each round's reps so you can compare to last week.
Run 4 circuits: 17 pushups, 25 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank, 60 seconds rest — write your reps down round by round.
tasks.why: Adding reps each week is how you build the muscle that increases your resting metabolism.

variants: Plan your meals for the next 3 days in MyFitnessPal — fill in breakfast, lunch, and dinner for days 16, 17, and 18 — verify calories and protein are on target for all 3.
Open MyFitnessPal and pre-log 3 full days of eating starting tomorrow — plan every meal, check the totals, adjust anything that's over budget.
Build a 3-day meal plan in MyFitnessPal right now for days 16-18 — every meal logged, every total verified against your calorie and protein targets.
tasks.why: Three-day planning cuts decision fatigue and prevents off-plan eating on busy days.

variants: Eat the breakfast you planned yesterday in MyFitnessPal and confirm it's logged — follow the plan exactly.
Execute the breakfast from your 3-day plan, log it immediately in MyFitnessPal, and verify it matches what you planned.
Open MyFitnessPal and follow yesterday's breakfast plan — eat it, confirm the entry is accurate, and move to the next meal.
tasks.why: Following a pre-logged plan is a skill — execute it without modification today.

variants: Walk 30 minutes and use a pedometer app to count total steps — your target is 6,000 steps or more for the full day.
Do your 30-minute walk and check your total step count for the day by 6pm — push to reach 6,000 steps across the whole day.
Take your 30-minute walk today and use your phone's health app to check your full-day step count — aim for 6,000+ by bedtime.
tasks.why: Step counting reveals your true daily movement beyond just the dedicated walk.

variants: Identify the meal or time of day when you most often go over your calorie target and write down one specific rule to prevent it — then follow the rule today.
Find your weakest calorie moment in the day (late night, post-work, weekend evenings) and write one concrete rule to guard against it — enforce it today.
Look at your MyFitnessPal patterns and pinpoint when you blow the budget most often — write a specific pre-decision rule for that moment and test it today.
tasks.why: Identifying your highest-risk eating window lets you design a rule that prevents it automatically.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 4 rounds of 17 pushups, 30 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — log total reps for all exercises.
Complete 4 rounds: 17 pushups, 30 squats, 15 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — rest 60 seconds, track every rep and compare to day 15.
Run 4 circuits: 17 pushups, 30 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — rest 60 sec, write down every rep count to measure progress.
tasks.why: Increasing squat reps each session strengthens your largest muscle groups for better fat burning.

variants: Log every meal today in real time — no catching up at night — log each item before or immediately after eating it.
Commit to real-time logging today: every meal, every snack entered in MyFitnessPal within 5 minutes of eating it.
Today's rule: nothing goes in your mouth without going into MyFitnessPal first — log it before you eat it or within 2 minutes after.
tasks.why: Real-time logging is the most accurate form of tracking and prevents underestimating portions.

variants: Make a shopping list of 5 high-protein foods under $3 per serving and buy at least 2 of them today — log which ones you got.
Write a grocery list of 5 budget-friendly protein sources, rank them by cost, and go buy the top 2 — bring them home and log them in MyFitnessPal.
Identify 5 high-protein foods that cost under $3 per serving, write them down, and purchase at least 2 of them today to keep your protein supply stocked.
tasks.why: A stocked fridge with affordable protein removes the excuse to eat off-plan.

variants: Check your water intake for today right now — if you haven't had 32oz yet by midday, drink 16oz immediately.
Open MyFitnessPal's water tracker or your notes app and check today's water total — drink at least 16oz right now if you're under 32oz.
Verify your water intake for today: if it's before 2pm and you haven't had 32oz yet, drink 16oz of water right now.
tasks.why: Midday hydration checks prevent the 3pm energy crash that leads to snacking.

variants: Walk 30 minutes today and add 2 minutes of hill walking or stair climbing at any point during the walk.
Do your 30-minute walk and incorporate 2 minutes of the steepest incline you can find — stairs, a hill, a parking garage ramp.
Take your 30-minute walk and add 2 minutes of incline walking at any point — find stairs, a hill, or any elevation change.
tasks.why: Incline walking burns significantly more calories than flat walking at the same pace.

variants: Close today's MyFitnessPal diary before 8pm — log dinner early, account for any evening snacks, and confirm your protein and calorie totals.
Finish logging your entire day in MyFitnessPal before 8pm tonight — include dinner and any planned evening snacks, check both targets.
By 8pm today, have your complete diary closed in MyFitnessPal — log dinner before you eat it if needed, and verify protein and calorie numbers.
tasks.why: Closing the diary early prevents late-night eating from blowing your budget.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 4 rounds of 20 pushups, 30 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — this is your new rep target.
Complete 4 rounds: 20 pushups, 30 squats, 15 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — 20 pushups is your new standard, track every round.
Run 4 circuits: 20 pushups, 30 squats, 15 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — reaching 20 pushups marks a real strength milestone.
tasks.why: Hitting 20 pushups per round is a measurable strength milestone — own it.

variants: Plan and cook one meal-prep batch today: 4-5 portions of a protein + one vegetable — store them in containers in the fridge.
Spend 5 minutes prepping 4-5 portions of one protein and one vegetable — cook it, portion it into containers, refrigerate it.
Do a mini meal-prep right now: cook 4-5 servings of a protein source plus any vegetable, portion them out, and put them in the fridge for this week.
tasks.why: Batch cooking prevents the 'I have nothing ready' excuse that derails dietary consistency.

variants: Walk 30 minutes today and track both your step count and your total active minutes in your phone's health app — write both numbers down.
Do your 30-minute walk and at the end, check your phone's health summary for step count and active minutes — record both in your notes.
Complete your 30-minute walk, then pull up your phone's health app and note today's step count and total active time — log both numbers.
tasks.why: Tracking two movement metrics gives you a more accurate picture of daily activity level.

variants: Weigh yourself this morning and log the number in MyFitnessPal — compare your current weight to the day-7 and day-14 weigh-ins and note the trend.
Step on the scale, record in MyFitnessPal, and compare to your week 1 and week 2 numbers — write the 3-week trend.
Do this week's mid-weigh-in: scale, MyFitnessPal entry, comparison to day 7 and day 14 — is the trend heading in the right direction?
tasks.why: A mid-week weigh-in every few days smooths out water fluctuations and shows true direction.

variants: Log every meal today with exact gram weights for at least 2 meals — use a kitchen scale if you have one, or measure with cups.
Use a measuring tool for at least 2 meals today — kitchen scale or measuring cups — and enter exact weights into MyFitnessPal.
Today, weigh or measure your portions for at least 2 meals with actual measuring tools before logging in MyFitnessPal — no eyeballing.
tasks.why: Measured portions expose how far estimates deviate from reality — you need the data.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 4 rounds of 20 pushups, 30 squats, 16 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — add one lunge per leg versus day 19.
Complete 4 rounds: 20 pushups, 30 squats, 16 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — one extra lunge rep per leg is today's increment.
Run 4 circuits: 20 pushups, 30 squats, 16 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — the extra lunge rep is small but the progression is real.
tasks.why: Micro-progressions add up — one more rep per session means 70 more reps over 10 weeks.

variants: Step on the scale and log your weight in MyFitnessPal — this is your 3-week weigh-in, compare to all prior weigh-ins and write the full trend.
Weigh in this morning, enter it in MyFitnessPal, and note your weight trajectory across day 1, day 7, day 14, and today.
Do your 3-week weigh-in: scale, MyFitnessPal, then write out your full weight line from day 1 to today — is the slope downward?
tasks.why: Three weeks of data reveals the actual trend beneath daily water-weight fluctuations.

variants: Review your MyFitnessPal diary for the past 7 days: write down your average daily protein, average daily calories, and how many days you hit both targets.
Pull up MyFitnessPal's week 3 report and record: average calories, average protein, and the number of days you closed the diary — compare to week 2.
Audit week 3 in MyFitnessPal right now — compute your 7-day averages for calories and protein, count your diary-closed days, and note your improvement from week 2.
tasks.why: Week 3 review shows whether your system is improving — compare to week 2 numbers, not just targets.

variants: Walk 30 minutes and use this walk to listen to a 5-minute podcast or audio on nutrition — then apply one takeaway to tonight's meal.
Do your 30-minute walk and, when you get back, write one concrete thing you're going to change in your eating based on what you've learned in week 3.
Complete your 30-minute walk, then write in your notes app: one eating habit you built, one habit you haven't locked in, and one specific fix for this week.
tasks.why: Week 3 reflection identifies the gap between what you know and what you're doing.

variants: Pre-log all 3 meals for today in MyFitnessPal before you eat the first one — see the full-day calorie and protein picture before the day starts.
Open MyFitnessPal this morning and fill in your plan for all 3 meals before eating anything — know your numbers in advance.
Before your first meal today, have all 3 meals entered in MyFitnessPal — plan the full day upfront so every decision is already made.
tasks.why: Full-day pre-logging gives you real-time budget awareness throughout the day.

variants: Do today's strength circuit: 4 rounds of 20 pushups, 30 squats, 18 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — beat the day-20 lunge count.
Complete 4 rounds: 20 pushups, 30 squats, 18 lunges per leg, 60-sec plank — 2 extra lunges per leg is today's progression.
Run 4 circuits: 20 pushups, 30 squats, 18 lunges each leg, 60-second plank — you're tracking the lunge progression from day 5 all the way here.
tasks.why: Consistent incremental progression over weeks builds real functional strength.

variants: Calculate half your bodyweight in ounces — that's your daily water target — and track every glass you drink today against that number.
Find your water target: take your weight in pounds, divide by 2, that's your daily ounce goal — track every glass in MyFitnessPal or notes today.
Work out your personal water goal today (bodyweight in lbs ÷ 2 = daily oz target), write it in your notes, and track every glass until you hit it.
tasks.why: A personalized water target is more effective than the generic 8-glasses advice.

variants: Walk 35 minutes today — 5 more minutes than your standard walk — and track the step count.
Bump today's walk to 35 minutes, brisk pace throughout, and log both the duration and step count when you finish.
